Module 1 · Section 1

What is an LLM?

A Large Language Model is an AI trained on massive text data to predict and generate language — one token at a time.

Next token prediction Text in → Text out Extraction Expansion Temperature Context window
Text input → statistical pattern learning → text prediction
The core idea

One job.
Done repeatedly.

Every LLM predicts the next token based on all previous tokens. There is no reasoning — only pattern completion at massive scale.

Input
Text in
your prompt
→
LLM
Predict next token
repeated until done
→
Output
Text out
the response
Key insight
The entire interface is text in → text out. Everything else is how you craft the input.
The only interface: Text In → LLM → Text Out
How it works

One token
at a time

The model does not generate the full sentence at once — it picks one token, then uses it to predict the next.

Step through →
The model reads the input and predicts: what is the most likely next token?
What is a token?
~0.75 words. fine-tuning = 3 tokens. 1,000 words ≈ 1,333 tokens.
The model assigns probabilities to all candidate next tokens
Task classification

Extraction
vs Expansion

Every LLM task either shrinks text or grows it. This shapes your model choice, temperature, and evaluation.

Extraction
Less text out
In"My order never arrived."
Outshipping_issue
Expansion
More text out
In"Write an apology email."
OutDear customer, we sincerely...

Click cards to cycle through examples

Extraction = smart filter · Expansion = intelligent writer
Real-world examples

Which tasks belong where?

Knowing the task type helps you choose the right model, temperature, and evaluation metric.

Extract
Named Entity Recognition (NER)
In: "Dr. Sarah Johnson from MIT..." → Out: [PER] Dr. Sarah Johnson [ORG] MIT
Extract
Sentiment classification
In: "Great product, really happy!" → Out: positive
Extract
Intent routing
In: "I want to cancel my subscription." → Out: billing_cancel
Expand
Translation
In: "The model works." → Out: "Model çalışıyor."
Expand
Code generation
In: "Write a Python JSON loader." → Out: def load_json(path): ...
Expand
Email writing
In: "Reply to a delayed order complaint." → Out: Dear customer, we sincerely...
Key parameters

Temperature & context window

Two settings every NLP engineer must understand before the first API call.

Temperature — controls randomness
0.0
temperature = 0.0 → always the same output. Use this for all NLP tasks: NER, classification, routing, structured extraction.
Context window
200K
tokens (~150K words)
1 token ≈
¾ word
not a full word
Model today
claude-sonnet-4-6
balanced speed + quality
Rule of thumb
Always use temperature=0 for NLP tasks. Only raise it when output variation is intentional.
